name: Firebase
summary: Most complete all-in-one starter if you want auth, database, storage, and deploy-friendly tooling in one ecosystem.
cons: meaningful ecosystem lock-in
pricing can feel fuzzy as usage grows
payments still require outside integration
recommended_for: builders who want fewer stack decisions
notes: Best all-in-one starter for getting moving without assembling too many parts.

name: Vercel + Supabase
summary: A practical modern default for web apps: clean frontend deploys paired with strong auth, database, and storage.
pros: excellent fit for modern React-style apps
cons: split stack means more decisions
pricing and responsibility live in two places
not truly all-in-one
avoid_if: you want one dashboard for everything
notes: Strong modern combo starter and likely the most balanced web-app default.

name: Render
summary: Feels closer to a conventional full-stack host, with less frontend-specialized polish but solid simplicity.
pros: easier to understand than cloud-sprawl setups
cons: auth is not first-class
less optimized for frontend-first workflows
not as integrated as all-in-one app platforms
recommended_for: builders who want app hosting without edge complexity
avoid_if: you want batteries-included auth and storage
notes: A sensible full-stack host if you value straightforward deployment over trendiness.

name: Railway
summary: A builder-friendly deployment platform with a pleasant workflow, especially for app backends and services.
pros: good for shipping real app services quickly
cons: not a complete product stack
fewer built-in primitives than Firebase or Supabase
long-term confidence can feel lower than bigger incumbents
recommended_for: builders who want less ceremony than cloud providers
avoid_if: you want an obvious all-in-one answer
notes: Good full-stack host, but not the easiest single recommendation for auth-heavy starters.

name: Cloudflare
summary: Extremely capable edge-first platform with broad primitives, but not always the lowest-friction starter path.
pros: strong fit for modern web delivery
cons: more platform-shaped thinking required
auth and product-layer conveniences are not the core story
can be overkill for simple starter builds
recommended_for: builders who specifically want edge-first power
avoid_if: you just want the simplest obvious MVP stack
notes: Powerful, but better when you know why you want it.

name: Vercel
summary: Excellent frontend-first deployment experience, but incomplete as a single answer for auth, database, and billing-heavy apps.
pros: great fit for content-heavy or frontend-led apps
cons: not a complete application stack by itself
you still need database/auth choices
cost clarity can get less obvious at scale
avoid_if: you want one stack choice that covers the whole app
notes: Great platform, but usually better as part of a combo than as the whole answer.

name: Supabase
summary: A strong backend platform layer for modern apps, especially when you want Postgres, auth, and storage with good developer ergonomics.
pros: portable-feeling relative to more closed ecosystems
cons: not a standalone hosting answer
frontend hosting still needs a partner
edge/server logic story is good but not everything
recommended_for: builders who want a real database and auth fast
teams pairing with Vercel or Cloudflare
avoid_if: you want frontend deployment included in the same choice
notes: Excellent building block, best understood as part of a stack decision.

name: Cloudflare + Supabase
summary: A compelling combo if you want Cloudflare’s edge model plus a saner auth/database layer from Supabase.
pros: strong auth and database without giving up edge reach
very strong PWA and performance fit
cons: more moving parts than simpler starters
requires comfort with platform decisions
not the easiest path for a first build
avoid_if: you want the lowest cognitive load
notes: Potentially excellent, but not as effortlessly starter-friendly as Vercel + Supabase.

name: Netlify
summary: Still solid for frontend deployment, but no longer feels like the automatic default for richer app stacks.
pros: works well for static and lightly dynamic projects
cons: not a full app stack by itself
less obvious for auth/database-heavy products
weaker starter story compared with stronger combos
avoid_if: you want a modern all-in-one app recommendation
notes: Solid contender, just not the easiest default answer today.

name: Vercel + Firebase
summary: Combines excellent frontend deployment with Firebase’s batteries-included backend services.
pros: strong frontend workflow plus strong product primitives
very good auth and storage story
can move fast with a small team
cons: still split across two systems
Firebase lock-in remains real
can be more stack than you need if Firebase alone is enough
recommended_for: builders who love Vercel on the frontend but want Firebase backend convenience
avoid_if: you want portability or simpler all-in-one ownership
notes: Strong combo, though arguably less clean than either Firebase alone or Vercel + Supabase.
